coredata.dc:title: Yolk-shell nanospheres based silane-functionalized carbon dots: Designed synthesis and application in highly selective determination of aflatoxin biosynthesis-related genes nor-1 in cereal crops
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yaqi
Yu, Tao
Peng, Shuangfeng
Sun, Qingyue
Kong, Dezhao
Liu, Chang
Zhang, Qi
Shi, Qiaoqiao
Chen, Yong
coredata.dc:description: This study presented advanced monitoring approaches for the aflatoxin gene cluster in Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus, which are effective tools for controlling aflatoxin proliferation in the food chain. A novel approach involved a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on yolk-shell nanospheres (bCDs@SiO2@gQDs). The probe's fluorescence was activated by DNA hybridization with the modified bCDs@SiO2@gQDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), functioning through donor–acceptor pairs. The exceptional properties of the gQDs and the high FRET efficiency of this system established a functionalized platform for DNA labeling. When the nor-1 gene was present, FRET between the gQDs and AuNPs triggered fluorescence quenching, enabling nor-1 gene detection. The yolk-shell nanostructure of the fluorescent nanoprobes provided an internal reference, reducing experimental errors due to background noise, instrumentation factors, and probe concentration. This allowed for accurate and highly sensitive quantitative determination of the nor-1 gene. The ratiometric fluorescence assay demonstrated a broad linear range (0.1–100 nM) and a low detection limit (12.83 pM) for the nor-1 gene. Tests on non-contaminated maize samples spiked with various concentrations of the nor-1 gene achieved recovery rates between 95.43 % and 101.46 %. This biosensor represents a significant advancement in food safety, offering a simple, rapid, and sensitive method for monitoring agricultural products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yolk-shell nanospheres
Ratiometric fluorescence
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Nor-1 genes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe fabrication and the detection process
The sensing mechanism and feasibility of the fabricated biosensor
Characterization of bCDs, gQDs and AuNPs
Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescence biosensor
Analytical application in spiked maize samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31901799 , 32001804 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021 M692370 ), the JUST Emerging Science and Technology Innovation Team Grant (No. 1182921902 ), the Jiangsu Dual Innovation Talent Program (No. 1184902001 ), and the Jiangsu University of Science and Technology Research Start-up Fund (No. 1182932001 , 1182922001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Gel electrophoresis of the process of nor-1 genes determination. Lane 1: DNA marker 1; lane 2: empty lane; lane 3: nor-1 genes; lane 4: Probe1; lane 5: Probe2; lane 6: Probe1 and Probe2; lane 7: nor-1 genes, Probe1 and Probe2.

TEM images of (A) bCDs, (B) gQDs, (C) bCDs@SiO2 and (D) bCDs@SiO2@gQDs, (E) UV–vis spectra of bCDs, gQDs, bCDs@SiO2 and bCDs@SiO2@gQDs, (F) FL spectra of bCDs (blue line), gQDs (green line), bCDs@SiO2 (red line) and bCDs@SiO2@gQDs (magenta line). (Illustrations show photographs of aqueous solutions of bCDs@SiO2, gQDs and bCDs@SiO2@gQDs in visible light (a - c) and UV light (e - g)). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS high-resolution spectra of full pattern (A), C 1 s (B), N 1 s (C), O 1 s (D), Si 2p (E), Cd 3d (F) and Te 3d (G) of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs.
Effects of pH and volume ratio of probe for the fluorescent intensity of gQDs.

(A) Responses of ratiometric fluorescent sensor to different concentrations of nor-1 genes, (B) liner fitting of different concentrations of nor-1 genes, (C) specificity of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor.
Schematic illustration sensing mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence system for the determination of nor-1 genes.

head.ce:title: Yolk-shell nanospheres based silane-functionalized carbon dots: Designed synthesis and application in highly selective determination of aflatoxin biosynthesis-related genes nor-1 in cereal crops

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Grain Science and Technology
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presented advanced monitoring approaches for the aflatoxin gene cluster in Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus, which are effective tools for controlling aflatoxin proliferation in the food chain. A novel approach involved a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on yolk-shell nanospheres (bCDs@SiO2@gQDs). The probe's fluorescence was activated by DNA hybridization with the modified bCDs@SiO2@gQDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), functioning through donor–acceptor pairs. The exceptional properties of the gQDs and the high FRET efficiency of this system established a functionalized platform for DNA labeling. When the nor-1 gene was present, FRET between the gQDs and AuNPs triggered fluorescence quenching, enabling nor-1 gene detection. The yolk-shell nanostructure of the fluorescent nanoprobes provided an internal reference, reducing experimental errors due to background noise, instrumentation factors, and probe concentration. This allowed for accurate and highly sensitive quantitative determination of the nor-1 gene. The ratiometric fluorescence assay demonstrated a broad linear range (0.1–100 nM) and a low detection limit (12.83 pM) for the nor-1 gene. Tests on non-contaminated maize samples spiked with various concentrations of the nor-1 gene achieved recovery rates between 95.43 % and 101.46 %. This biosensor represents a significant advancement in food safety, offering a simple, rapid, and sensitive method for monitoring agricultural products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins are secondary metabolites produced by a various genera of filamentous fungi [1] and exist widely in cereal crops such as wheat, peanuts, rice, maize et al [2–4]. Due to the super toxicity and difficulty of degradation of aflatoxin, it was considered as one of mycotoxins for human health risks and caused substantial financial losses [5–7]. The process of aflatoxin biosynthesis involves a series of oxidation–reduction reactions [8] and requires an abundance of enzymes encoded by at least 30 genes clustered in a gene cluster of about 82 kB [9]. The nor-1 gene encodes a ketoreductase that translates norsolorinic acid (NOR) to avilufin (AVN) [10,11], which is a critical step in the aflatoxin biosynthetic pathway. As a result, the nor-1 gene is considered a decisive gene in the synthesis of aflatoxin. If aflatoxin-producing genes can be detected before they are expressed, it is possible to effectively control aflatoxins from entering the food chain.

The fluorescence intensity of traditional fluorescent probes in real application scenarios is highly susceptible to fluctuations in light source intensity, changes in microenvironmental viscosity, probe concentration and photobleaching effects, resulting in reduced detection accuracy [12,13]. However, using two or more emission wavelengths can account for environmental variability and provide a built-in self-calibration, which makes the results more accurate and reliable. The ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths is directly related to the analyte’s concentration, providing an effective internal reference system [14,15]. Thus, ratiometric fluorescent probes can effectively contribute to the sensitivity and accuracy of analyte detection through their self-calibration function, not only for the assay of trace analytes in complex samples, but also for the visual detection of analytes [16]. Ratiometric fluorescent probes have been applied in various fields including ions (metal ions or anions), small molecules (trinitrotoluene), pH sensing, biomolecules sensing (proteins and nucleic acids) [17–20].

In this work, we proposed a ratiometric fluorescence detection to determine the aflatoxin biosynthesis-related genes nor-1 for the first time based on SiO2 loaded-QDs as donors and AuNPs as acceptor. Blue silane-functionalized carbon dots (bCDs) were used as an in-house guide and green-emitting CdTe QDs (gQDs) bound to Probe1 as the responsive fluorophore. In the presence of nor-1 gene, it was hybridized with Probe1 labeled with bCDs@SiO2@gQDs and Probe2 coupled with AuNPs, bringing gQDs and AuNPs close to each other. The fluorescence of gQDs was quenched due to the Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between gQDs and AuNPs, while the bCDs maintained stable and strong fluorescence. In the absence of nor-1 gene due to electrostatic repulsion of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs and Probe2 coupled with AuNPs, they remained at a certain distance and the fluorescence of bCDs and gQDs was not significantly affected. This proposed ratiometric fluorescence probe was used for the sensitive and selective detection of nor-1 gene. In addition, this designed probe was employed in the determination of real maize samples with excellent results, indicating that it has the potential to be a viable tool for the identification of nor-1 genes in the storage and processing of agricultural products.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as purchased without further treatment. Tellurium power, Cadmium chloride (CdCl2), Natrium borohidrida (NaBH4), Natrium sitrat monohidrat (C6H5Na3O7·H2O), Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), HAuCl4·4H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Poly-DADMAC (PDDA), Tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. Ultrapure water (electrical resistivity 18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared daily from water passed by a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore ltd., Bedford, USA). All the DNA oligonucleotides utilized in this work were provided and purified by Sangon (Shanghai, China). The DNA sequences were heated to 95 °C for 10 min and then cooled to room temperature for at least 6 h for later use. The detailed sequences information for all the DNAs were shown in Table S1.

All fluorescence spectra of the samples were obtained on spectrofluorometer FS5. The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Data were collected in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The size and topography of bCDs@SiO2 and bCDs@SiO2@gQDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Talos F200X). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurement was carried out with a Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Co., Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of NCQDs were acquired by using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE powder X-ray diffractometer (Karlsruhe, Germany). Elemental and functional groups analysis is performed on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS-Ultra DLD). DLS (dynamic light scattering) analyzed with zeta potential instrument Malvern Instruments Ltd (Zetasizer Ver. 7.11, MAL1032660, England) for zeta potential and size distribution of AuNPs. The Malvern Zetasizer® series of instruments are widely popular within university graduates and has emerged as gradual evolution of the original Malvern Correlator® marketed.

Silane-functionalized CDs (bCDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method [21]. In brief, 0.4 g of trisodium citrate (carbon source) was dissolved in 8 mL of ultrapure water under ultrasound, and 8 mL of APTES (functionalization reagent) was then injected into the solution and stirred with N2 for 15 min. The obtained precursor solution was poured into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and reacted at 200 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. Pure SiCDs can be easily obtained through extraction by weakly polar solvents such as petroleum ether or n-hexane [8], and placed at 4 °C for later use.

Green CdTe quantum dots (gQDs) were synthesized according to the previous work with some modifications [22,23]. 0.0449 g of NaBH4 and 0.0638 g of tellurium power were mixed with 5 mL of ultrapure water for 15 min to generate NaHTe under N2. Afterwards, 0.114 g of CdCl2 and 100 μL of MPA were dissolved into 50 mL of ultrapure water, the pH of the above solution was adjusted to 10 by 1 M NaOH. Subsequently, it was introducted into a three-necked with N2 bubbing for 15 min, the frshly-prepared NaHTe was mixed with the precursor under and heated to 100 °C for 30 min under oil bath magnetic stirring. After the purification step, the as-obtained gQDs were redispersed into 10 mL of ultrapure water for the later use.

AuNPs with the average size of 25 nm were synthesised by one-step reduction of HAuCl4 with sodium citrate in solution [24]. Before the experiment, all-glass instruments were soaked in aqua regia for 12 h, cleaned with ultrapure water, and dried. The detailed process was as follows: 50 mL of HAuCl4 (1 mM) was added to a three-necked flask, heated to boiling, and refluxed under magnetic stirring, and then 5 mL of sodium citrate solution (1.0 wt%) was quickly injected. The color of the solution quickly changed from light yellow to wine, and continued to reflux for 10 min. The solution cooled to room temperature naturally, was filtered by 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and stored at 4 °C for the later use.

First of all, the bCDs@SiO2 was prepared by the stöber method [22,25]. The specific operation process was listed as follow: 0.1 g of bCDs were dispersed into 5 mL of ultrapure water and mixed with 30 mL EtOH for 5 min under dark condition, and 100 μL of APTES was transferred to flask and the mixture was stirred for 6 h at room temperature. Then, l mL of NH3·H2O was added into the flask and mixed by constantly stirring evenly, 10.5 mL of ethanol / TEOS (v:v = 20:1) mixture was dropped into the mixed dispersion slowly with overnight reaction. After that, the obtained bCDs@SiO2 was washed firstly by pure water following by ethanol for another 3 times with centrifugation to remove unreacted agents. Eventually, the precipitations were dried by vacuum-drying for 24 h.

To form core–shell nanoparticles, 100 mg of bCDs@SiO2 was firstly dispersed in 40 mL of PDDA (2.5 mg / mL, 10 mM PBS buffer, pH 10) for 3 h under stirring constantly in dark environment. After centrifugal washing, the PDDA modified bCDs@SiO2 was redispersed into 20 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of the resultant gQDs (1.2 mL). After stirring for 3 h and being washed, the bCDs@SiO2@gQDs core–shell nanospheres were collected and dispersed in 5 mL of deionized H2O for further use.

The Probe1 was functionalized on the surface of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs according to the previous work [26,27]. Firstly, 2.5 mL of the prepared bCDs@SiO2@gQDs was dispersed in 2 mL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and mixed with 40 μL of EDC (400 mM, in PBS buffer) and 20 μL of NHS (200 mM, in PBS buffer) for 1 h to activate carboxyl on the surface of gQDs. Subsequently, 20 μL of Probe1 (100 μM) was added. The above reactants were oscillated at room temperature for overnight. Unfunctionalized Proeb1 was removed by centrifugation and the obtained precipitations were washed with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) for three times. The bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1 was redispersed in 5 mL of PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) for later use.

Probe2 was first incubated with TCEP referring reported protocol [28] to cleave the disulfide linkage and activate the thiol group. After that, the activated Proebe2 was mixed with the concentrated AuNPs for 20 min with shaking at room temperature. The obtained solution was mixed with 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4) containing 1 % sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and the final concentrations of phosphate and SDS were brought to 0.01 M and 0.01 %, respectively. The solution was sonicated for 10 s, and incubated for overnight. Subsequently, using 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2,2.5 M NaCl respectively to conduct salt aging process, every step intervaled 30 min, making the final concentration to 0.5 M [29]. After the salt aging, the mixture was shaken at room temperature overnight. To remove uncoupled Probe1, the solution was centrifuged at 12500 rpm for 10 min and washed by PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) three times, the prepared AuNPs-Probe2 was dispersed in 5 mL of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe fabrication and the detection process
ce:section.ce:para: 20 μL bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1, 120 μL of AuNPs-Probe2 were mixed with 50 μL different concentrations of nor-1 genes containing solutions. Making a total volume to 350 μL by using PBS buffer, and then the solution was incubated for 1 h at 43 °C to form the ratiometric fluorescence probe of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1/nor-1/AuNPs-Probe2 bioconjugations. Different concentration of nor-1 gene was introduced to this system for observation of ratiometric fluorescence response to achieve the target detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism and feasibility of the fabricated biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Scheme 1 , bCDs@SiO2@gQDs and AuNPs were coupled to the ends of Probe1 and Probe2, respectively, chosen as fluorescence energy donor and acceptor through amide condensation and Au-S bonding. The crucial elements affecting the FRET performance of the biosensor are the distance between the fluorescent donor and acceptor molecules, the extent of spectral overlap, and the dipole direction of the donor and acceptor. Based on this theory, Probe1 and Probe2 was designed elaborately and labeled with bCDs@SiO2@gQDs and AuNPs, respectively. In the absence of the nor-1 genes, both bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1 and AuNPs-Probe2 carry a negative charge and do not hybridize with each other, causing bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1 to emit strong fluorescent intensity (FL “on”). In the presence of the nor-1 genes, nor-1 genes co-hybridized with bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1 and AuNPs-Probe2 to bring energy pairs closer together to 8.84 nm, which triggered FRET to effectively occur from gQDs on the surface of bCDs@SiO2 to AuNPs. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity at 517 nm of gQDs was decreased sharply (FL “off”) and the fluorescence intensity at 446 nm of bCDs@SiO2 was no significant change due to the protection of SiO2 for bCDs. Using gQDs and AuNPs as FRET donor–acceptor pairs, the nor-1 gene can be detected bytesting the fluorescence intensity ratio of gQDs and bCDs.

To verify the feasibility of the proposed assay, gel electrophoresis was used to demonstrate the connection between bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1 and AuNPs-Probe2. As shown in Fig. 1 , lane 1 to 7 represented the DNA marker, empty lane, nor-1 genes, Probe1, Proeb2, Probe1/Probe2 and Probe1/nor-1/Probe2, respectively. Two bands can be observed in overlapping shadows in lane 6, corresponding to Probe1 and Probe2, no extra band appeared. In contrast, a new band presented with a significant increase in molecular weight in lane 7, which proved nor-1 genes matched with Proeb1 and Probe2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of bCDs, gQDs and AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
bCDs and gQDs were obtained by one -step pyrolysis of trisodium citrate and 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES). As can be seen in Fig. 2 A, the prepared bCDs are well dispersed and subspherical with an average-sized diameter of 2.5 nm, which is in accordance with previous reports. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show the particle size and shape of the synthesised gQDs as shown in Fig. 2B. It is clear from the figure that the relatively uniformly distributed gQDs appear spherical in shape with an average size of approximately 1.5 nm. The lattice edges of gQDs with high crystallinity can be seen through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images with edge spacing distances of approximately 0.3199 nm. It can be noticed that the AuNPs obtained with good water solubility and dispersion have a nearly spherical shape and a diameter of 24 nm. (Fig. S1A). Fig. S1B shows the UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra of the bCDs and curve Abs shows the high energy UV absorption near 234 nm and 340 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of the sp2 hybridised carbon and the n-π* transition at the carbon lattice edge, respectively [30]. The excitation and emission peaks of the bCDs are located at 344 nm and 445 nm respectively. bCDs exhibit yellowish and blue fluorescence under light (photo a in the inset) and under UV light (photo b in the inset). (Fig. S1B). Besides, Fig. S1C illustrated the absorption and fluorescent [31] spectra of gQDs, which were very sharp. According to the literature, the size can be calculated by the empirical fitting functions of the curves [32]:
D =
9.812 ×
10
- 7
λ
3
-
1.7147 ×
10
- 3
λ
2
+ 1.0064 λ - 194.84
. (D (nm) is the size of gQDs, and λ was the wavelength of the first excitonic absorption peak of the gQDs). The results were consistent with TEM. The gQDs showed yellow color and green fluorescence under light (photo a in the inset) and UV lamp (photo b in the inset) (Fig. S1C). Fig. S2C shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of gQDs, showing diffraction peaks of (111), (220) and (311) for gQDs respectively [33].

The formation process of yolk-shell structure of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs was carried out using stöber method and electrostatic adsorption, resulting in the separation of the components in different spaces of the nanoprobe. The morphological structure of bCDs@SiO2 and bCDs@SiO2@gQDs was characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2C, the bCDs@SiO2 are well dispersed and have small sizes with a narrow size distribution (70.09 ± 0.3 nm). Compared to bCDs@SiO2, the size of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs (Fig. 2D) increased slightly, with a size distribution of 75.92 ± 0.6 nm, which is attributed to the coating of gQDs on the surface of bCDs@SiO2. Fig. 2E shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of bCDs, gQDs, bCDs@SiO2, and bCDs@SiO2@gQDs, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs show the characteristic absorption peaks of bCDs and gQDs near 240 and 476 nm, respectively, which confirm the successful encapsulation of bCDs with silica nanospheres and loading of gQDs on the surface of silica nanospheres. The as-prepared aqueous suspension of bCDs@SiO2 showed a milky white color under daylight (photograph a, inset of Fig. 2F) and bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (photograph e, inset of Fig. 2F), consistent with the properties of free SiO2 nanospheres. Moreover, when gQDs were coated on the surface of bCDs@SiO2, the color of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs changed from milky white to yellow under daylight and from blue to green under UV light, which is similar to the color of the aqueous solution of gQDs. Upon excitation at 365 nm, the resulting bCDs@SiO2@gQDs nanospheres exhibited dual-emission at 445 nm and 521 nm, respectively.

The formation of bCDs@SiO2@gQD was confirmed by infrared spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra of bCDs, gQD, bCDS@SiO2@gQD and bCDs@SiO2@gQD-Probe1 are shown in Fig. S2A and B, respectively. In Fig. S2A, the absorption peak is 1135 cm−1 and the absorption peak is 920 cm−1. Using sodium citrate as raw material, bCDs was prepared by carbonization of sodium citrate and water vapor, and bCDs was prepared by acylation of amine group in APTES and ester group of carboxyl group on sodium citrate carbon. The feasibility of this method was verified by infrared spectroscopy [34]. Its peak value is 1679 cm−1, which is caused by secondary amide (C = O). The 3457 cm−1 peak and the 1586 cm−1 peak are caused by the flexural vibration caused by secondary imide R-NH-R [35]. On this basis, the interaction between it and APTES was further studied, and the interaction between it and APTES was determined through the interaction between two different molecules. IR spectra show that the elastic modulus of carboxylic acid molecule is 1403 cm−1 and the asymmetric modulus is 1558 cm−1. The characteristic peak on 1640 cm−1 is the expansion oscillation of (C = O) [36,37], and the characteristic peak on 3428 cm−1 is caused by –OH expansion oscillation. The results showed that the bCDs@SiO2@gQD composites were successfully prepared and the characteristic peaks of bCDs@SiO2@gQD were obtained. The X-ray diffraction pattern of bCDS@SiO2@gQD is shown in Fig. S2D below. Compared with bCDs, bCDs@SiO2@gQD shows an obvious redshift at 2θ = 21.8 [38], which provides new evidence for the successful preparation of bCDs@SiO2@gQD.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on bCDs@SiO2@gQDs with the aim to further support confirmation of the surface elemental component. The combination of bCDs, SiO2, and gQDs was confirmed by the distinctive peaks for carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), sulfur (S), silicon (S), cadmium (Cd), and tellurium (Te) in the XPS overview spectrum (Fig. 3 A). The high-resolution spectra of each element, which were examined in greater depth, provided further supporting data. The high-resolution carbon spectra (C1s, Fig. 3B) revealed four peaks at 284.6, 284.9, 285.5, 286.3, and 287.8 eV, which, respectively, correspond to the C-H, C = C, C-N, C-C, and C = O groups [39,40]. Three distinctive peaks at 399.5, 399.9, and 401.5 eV, which correspond to N-H bonds, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively, were clearly visible in the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 3C) [41]. This suggests that the bCDs in the bCDs@SiO2@gQDs nanospheres are the primary source of the elements C and N. Peaks associated to the Si-O-Si, Si-OH, and C = O groups were seen in the oxygen high-resolution spectrum (O1s, Fig. 3D). Four characteristic peaks at 101.2, 102.1, 102.6, and 103.4 eV, which are referred to as the Si(-O)1, Si(-O)2, Si(-O)3, and Si(-O)4 abbreviated systems [42], were seen in the Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 3E). Two contributions to the Cd 3d spectrum (Fig. 3F) that may be attributed to Cd ions are 3d3/2 and 3d5/2, which are positioned at 404 and 411.5 eV, respectively. Te3d3/2 and Te3d5/2 have tellurium (Te) binding energies of 572 and 582.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 3G). The bCDs' diffraction pattern (Fig. S2D) revealed a wide peak at 25.5°, supporting their amorphous nature and little graphitization. Peaks at around 25°, which correspond to the typical peak of amorphous silica, were also seen, however they migrated to a lesser angle as a result of doping with bCDs.

The attachment of gQDs to the surface of bCDs@SiO2 nanospheres was achieved electrostatically. bCDs@SiO2 nanospheres were surrounded by abundant hydroxyl groups and were negatively charged at neutral pH with a zeta potential of −50.83 mV (Fig. S3A), which shifted to a positive zeta potential of 38.7 mV upon adsorption of the cationic polyelectrolyte PDDA. The negatively charged surface of gQDs terminated by MPA was attached to the surface of bCDs@SiO2 by electrostatic self-assembly. The resulting bCDs@SiO2@gQDs hybrid spheres had a zeta potential of −13.3 mV, indicating that gQDs had combined with bCDs@SiO2 and that the surface of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs was negatively charged.

MPA-modified gQDs, rich in carboxyl groups on their surfaces, were condensed with amino-terminal modified Probe1 in the presence of EDC and NHS. Fluorescence and UV–vis spectra were used to study the properties of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1 and AuNPs-Probe2. Fig. S4A showed a minor red-shift in the emission of gQDs after conjugation with Probe1, as indicated by the emission of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs and bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1. Fig. S4B showed a new peak at around 260 nm for bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1, compared to bCDs@SiO2@gQDs, indicating the conjugation of Probe1 on bCDs@SiO2@gQDs. The colloidal gold showed a characteristic SPR peak at 525 nm (Fig. S4C), indicating good dispersion in solution. Upon coupling with Proeb2, the SPR band red-shifted due to the change in dielectric constant of the AuNPs. Additionally, after modification with DNA, the AuNPs showed increased negative charges (Fig. S3B). Dynamic light scattering experiments (Fig. S3C) showed a larger average hydrodynamic diameter of AuNPs-Probe2 (27 nm) compared to AuNPs (24 nm), possibly due to modification of the DNA layer on the gold nanoparticle surface. Fig. S4D showed well-overlapping spectra (over 60 % spectral overlap) between the fluorescence emission spectra of gQDs and the absorption spectra of AuNPs, providing excellent conditions for effective FRET.

The optimization of the analytical performance of this method was carried out by adjusting several key parameters and shown in Fig. 4 . One important factor was the pH of the solution, as the MPA ligands on the surface of the gQDs could be desorbed under inappropriate pH. From pH 5 to pH 7.4, the fluorescence intensity of the gQDs was decreasing, meanwhile, from pH 7.4 to pH 9, the fluorescence intensity was increasing. The fluorescence intensity of the gQDs was the lowest in pH 7.4. Considering there was the observed fluorescent signal, stability of bCDs@SiO2 and friendly environment. The pH 7.4 was chosen as the suitable pH for the biosensing platform. The volume ratio of bCDs@SiO2@gQDs-Probe1 and AuNPs-Probe2 was also crucial for effective fluorescence quenching of the gQDs. The fluorescence intensity of the gQDs decreased consistently as the volume ratio of the two probes increased until it reached a ratio of 6:1, which was therefore chosen as the optimal volume ratio for high-efficiency FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescence biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence biosensor was used to determine the concentration of nor-1 genes. The fluorescence intensity of gQDs decreased with the addition of nor-1 genes, indicating that FRET was occurring between gQDs and AuNPs. The fluorescence intensity of bCDs remained unchanged. A positive linear correlation was shown in Fig. 5 and observed between the ratio of gQDs and bCDs fluorescence intensity (F518/F446) and nor-1 genes concentration in the range of 0.1 to 100 nM, with a linear equation of F518/F446 = -0.02338C + 5.118 and a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.997. The limit of detection was calculated to be 12.83 pM. As shown in Table S2, this proposed ratiometric fluorescent biosensor displayed a wider linear range and lower LOD for nor-1 gene or other genes detection.

The selectivity of the nor-1 genes ratiometric fluorescence biosensor was tested by using penta-base mismatched target DNA (5mis), double-base mismatched target DNA (2mis), and random DNA sequence (Random). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of gQDs in the 5mis, 2mis and random DNA groups showed almost no fluctuation, while there was a significant decline in the fluorescence intensity of gQDs in the target group at the same concentration. This showed the high specificity of the proposed biosensor, as even double-mismatched target DNA could not trigger the FRET process. bCDs were found to have poor responsiveness towards all tested nor-1 genes at the same concentration, which made them suitable as a stable reference signal without the need for tedious and time-consuming encapsulation processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application in spiked maize samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability and reliability of the established method was proved in real samples. First of all, maize flours with positive Aspergillus flavus were incubated under 35℃ for different days and used to extract toxin-producing gene fragments by DNA extraction kits in 5 days, 10 days and 15 days. The result was shown in Fig. S5, with longer incubation time, the concentration of nor-1 gene was increased and caused stronger fluorescence quenching in this fabricated ratiometric fluorescent sensor. Meanwhile, this biosensor was also applied to determine the recoveries of standard spiking methods for three different concentrations of nor-1 gene in real maize samples. As illustrated in Table S3, the recoveries obtained from addition samples and relative standards ranged from 95.43 to 101.46 % and 1.1–7.5 %, respectively. The experimental results indicate that this ratiometric fluorescent biosensor can be applied to the analysis of the nor-1 gene in agricultural products.

In conclusion, a novel ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on bCDs@SiO2@gQDs with yolk-shell nanostructures and AuNPs were used as the fluorescence energy donor and acceptor pair for detection of nor-1 genes in maize samples was reported. The bCDs were encapsulated in SiO2 nanospheres, which avoided the aggregation of the bCDs efficiently and offered convenient surface derivations and specific surface area. Meanwhile, the coating of gQDs on SiO2 surface can solve the self-agglomeration of the ultra-small nanoparticles, which facilitated the amplification of fluorescence signal. The FRET system was achieved by immobilization of the amino-functionalized probe1 on bCDs@SiO2@gQDs and conjugation of the thiol-functionalized probe2 on AuNPs. The designed biosensing exhibited a wide linear range of 0.1–100 nM with a LOD of 12.83 pM. The fabricated biosensor was used to quantify the nor-1 gene in real samples with satisfactory recovery and high accuracy. This study not only presents a new and facile ratiometric fluorescence strategy for ultra-sensitive detection of the nor-1 gene, extending the potential of silica yolk-shell structures for application, but also provides an efficient way to facilitate early screening of aflatoxins from agricultural products to quantitative detection without the need for complex procedures.

Yaqi Li: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Tao Yu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shuangfeng Peng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Qingyue Sun: Data curation, Formal analysis. Dezhao Kong: Supervision. Chang Liu: Validation. Qi Zhang: Methodology. Qiaoqiao Shi: Methodology. Yong Chen: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31901799, 32001804), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021 M692370), the JUST Emerging Science and Technology Innovation Team Grant (No. 1182921902), the Jiangsu Dual Innovation Talent Program (No. 1184902001), and the Jiangsu University of Science and Technology Research Start-up Fund (No. 1182932001, 1182922001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.109929.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yaqi Li received her Ph.D. degree in Food Science and Engineering at Jiangsu University, as well as a visiting scholar at the Institute of Biological Information Processing, Bioelectronics (IBI-3), Jülich Research Center, a postdoc study at Advanced Technology Institute of Suzhou and School of Environmental Science and Engineering in Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. Her main research areas are the characteristic of nanomaterials, functionalization of bio-probes and the application of biosensors.
Tao Yu is pursuing his Master’s degree in Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, China. His research topic is synthesis of multicolor quantum dots to apply in biosensor.
Shuangfeng Peng is pursuing her Master’s degree in Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, China. Her working topics are preparation and application of nanomaterials for the sensing systems.

Qingyue Sun is pursuing her Master’s degree in Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, China. Her research interests relate to unconventional fluorescent materials: synthesis, structures, properties, and applications; design and assemble of nucleic acid.

Dezhao Kong received his Ph.D. degree in Jiangnan University, Chian. He is mainly engaged in the research of preparation of monoclonal antibody, eatablishment of immunoassay method, development of highly sensitive detection biodetection sensors.

Chang Liu received his Ph.D. degree in Southeast University, China. He received his Master’s degree in Anhui Normal University, China. His current scientific interests focus on the development of novel nanomaterials for bio-sensors and catalytic application.
Qiaoqiao Shi received her Ph.D. degree in Jiangnan University, Chian. She current scientific interests focus on the food safety rapid detection technology.

Yong Chen received his Ph.D. degree in Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, as well as a visiting scholar at the Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Massachusetts, a postdoc study at School of Environmental Science and Engineering in Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. His main research areas are the environmental photochemistry and migrationand transformation of new pollutants.
